text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, two bilateral direct inguinal hernias are immediately visible.
The surgeon creates a flap by dissecting the peritoneum to access the layers of the abdominal wall, preparing to position the mesh beneath the peritoneum.

The surgeon begins on the patient's left side, dissecting down to the pubic bone. The left-sided direct inguinal hernia is then reduced by pulling it out of the hernia space and freeing it from surrounding attachments, resulting in the hernia being completely reduced.
The surgeon reduces a small indirect inguinal hernia located around the spermatic cord structures, carefully freeing it from surrounding attachments.

The surgeon moves to the right side and creates a flap by dissecting the peritoneum to access the abdominal wall layers. The direct inguinal hernia is reduced and pulled out of the direct space, visibly completing the reduction.

The surgeon introduces mesh into the abdominal cavity to cover the hernia spaces on both sides, ensuring it overlaps across the pubic bone. Absorbable sutures are placed to fixate the mesh, preventing movement as it incorporates into the tissue.

The final surgical outcome shows all hernias reduced with the mesh securely in place, covering the hernia spaces from side to side. The surgeon closes the flaps, effectively concealing the mesh from the abdominal cavity, ensuring it is not exposed to the underlying bowel.
The surgeon shows the completely closed flap, with the mesh hidden beneath, ensuring the space is sealed off from the abdominal cavity. All sutures have been removed, leaving a smooth surface.
